title: MaxFitCalib-Bench: A Benchmark for LLM Calibration in Fitness Advice
keyTakeaways: We created 126 expert-crafted fitness advice questions across 8 categories, grounded in ACSM/NSCA/AHA position stands, each tagged with evidence uncertainty type.

Evaluating two models (DeepSeek-chat, Qwen-plus) revealed the precision illusion: 92.9% of outputs are Calibrated, but the dominant failure mode is Pseudo-precise (6.3%) — precise-sounding numbers without evidence support.
Pseudo-precise outputs cluster in safety-critical scenarios: 23.7% on mixed-evidence questions and 26.7% on insufficient-evidence questions.
At 6.3% per output, a typical user session of 10 advice statements carries a ~48% cumulative probability of encountering at least one Pseudo-precise response.
Calibration quality appears decoupled from factual accuracy — no significant UCS distribution difference between models despite their accuracy gap.
hciRelevance: Challenges the assumption that LLMs' primary risk is over-reliance — the bigger risk may be subtle misdirection through false precision that standard accuracy benchmarks cannot detect.

faq.a: Fitness was chosen as a testbed for its accessible, safety-critical characteristics shared with medical, legal, and financial advice. Fitness knowledge can be verified against ACSM and NSCA position stands, providing objective ground truth.

faq.q: Could the 0% overconfident result be a judge artifact?
faq.a: Yes — this is acknowledged as a key limitation. The LLM judge (GPT-5.5-instant) may preferentially classify outputs matching its own style as Calibrated. Human validation (target Cohen's κ ≥ 0.70) is planned and ongoing.

citation.apa: Guo, M. (2026). MaxFitCalib-Bench: A Benchmark for LLM Calibration in Fitness Advice. arXiv. https://gbx-max1220.github.io/maxguo.dev/research/measuring-ai-reliability/fitcalib-bench/

reproducible.quickStart: git clone ... && cd MaxFitCalib-Bench && pip install -r requirements.txt && python evaluation/run_benchmark.py

changelog.event: arXiv submission. 126 questions, two models, UCS evaluations complete.
UCS Engine (4-stage pipeline) implemented and validated on 252 responses
